title: Scripts to delete Files and Folders on Windows - Hexnode Help Center
content: Help Center Sample Script Repository Windows Category filter

title: Scripts to delete Files and Folders on Windows
content: When the number of files/folders in an organization increases, the administrators might need to delete outdated files. With more and more machines getting remote, admins find this task increasingly difficult. Hexnode lets admins handle this action viascriptson Windows devices. Supported Versions: The scripts given below will be supported on the following versions: Windows 10 v1607 (Pro, Enterprise, Education) Windows 11 (Pro, Enterprise, Education) The sample scripts provided below are adapted from third-party open-source sites.

title: Batch Script
content: E.g., To delete the file test.txt in the Desktop of the user Deborah,del C: Users Deborah Desktop test.txt

title: PowerShell Script
content:
E.g., To delete the file test.txt in the Desktop of the user Deborah,remove-item C: Users Deborah Desktop test.txt The admin can also delete files older than a specific number of days.The .bat script to delete .docx files that are older than 20 days from the location C: Users username Documents My Blogs is as follows:Batch script to delete files older than 20 days@echo off forfiles /p C: Users username Documents My Blogs /s /m .docx /d -20 /c cmd /c del @path echo Document files older than 20 days deleted
exit123@echooffforfiles/p C: Users username Documents My Blogs /s/m .docx/d-20/c cmd /c del @path echoDocumentfilesolderthan20daysdeletedpauseexit The.ps1 script to delete files older than 20 days is as follows:PowerShell script to delete files older than 20 daysWrite-Host Starting cleanup of old files
CutoffDate CurrentDate.AddDays( DaysThreshold)
files Get-ChildItem path -Recurse -Filter .bat Where-Object
Write-Host Deleted file: ( file.FullName)
Write-Host Cleanup completed. 123456789101112131415161718192021Write-Host Starting cleanup of old files Set target folder and age threshold path C: Scripting DaysThreshold -20 Calculate cutoff date CurrentDate Get-Date CutoffDate CurrentDate.AddDays( DaysThreshold) Find and delete files older than cutoff files Get-ChildItem path -Recurse -Filter .bat Where-Object _.LastWriteTime -lt CutoffDate foreach ( file in files) Remove-Item file.FullName -ForceWrite-Host Deletedfile: ( file.FullName) Write-Host Cleanupcompleted.

title: Batch Script
content: E.g., To delete a folder test in the Desktop of the user Deborah,rmdir /Q/S C: Users Deborah Desktop test /S deletes all the files from the folder. /Q makes sure that the user is not asked for confirmation for deleting the folder.

title: Powershell Script
content: E.g., To delete a folder test in the Desktop of the user Deborah, remove-item C: Users Deborah Desktop test -recurse The recurse attribute ensures that all the files inside the folder are deleted. It is recommended to manually validate the script execution on a system before executing the action in bulk. Hexnode will not be responsible for any damage/loss to the system on the behavior of the script. Share on: Share on:

title: Enable or Disable Microsoft Accounts on Windows Devices via PowerShell - Hexnode Help Center
content: Help Center Sample Script Repository Windows Category filter

title: Enable or Disable Microsoft Accounts on Windows Devices via PowerShell
content: Microsoft Accounts allow users to synchronize personal data, settings, and cloud services across devices. However, in a corporate environment, MSAs can pose security risks, such as unmanaged data synchronization or unauthorized access. This guide provides PowerShell scripts to centrally manage the accessibility of Microsoft Accounts. By utilizing Hexnode UEM sExecute Custom Scriptaction, administrators can remotelyenableordisableMSAs to maintain full control over local authentication methods.

title: System Requirements
content: Operating Systems:Windows 10 and Windows 11 (Pro, Enterprise, and Education editions). Permissions:Target devices must be enrolled in Hexnode UEM. Environment:Devices must be online during script execution. The sample scripts provided are adapted from open-source repositories. Administrators must review and execute them on a test machine before initiating a bulk deployment across the production environment.

title: Script to Disable Microsoft Accounts on Windows
content: This script executes a comprehensive scan to identify all active Microsoft Accounts and programmatically restricts them. It includes a safety check to ensure it only targets Microsoft Accounts to prevent a lockout risk. The script initially checks for a local administrator, and only if one is found, it disables all Microsoft Accounts.

title: Script To Enable Microsoft Accounts on windows
content: This script identifies and enables all currently disabled Microsoft Accounts on your windows device.

title: Script Execution steps
content: Save the above codes as a.ps1file and deploy it using theExecute Custom Scriptaction available within the Hexnode UEM portal. Go toManage Devices Select your Windows device. SelectActions Execute Custom Script. Upload the saved script file and click Execute.

title: Verifying the results
content: Once the script is executed, the results are captured and displayed directly under device sAction Historytab. Administrators can view the results by clicking on theShow Outputbutton, which returns a definitive audit trail of all Microsoft Accounts that were modified. This non-intrusive verification process ensures that changes are confirmed without interrupting the end-user productivity or requiring physical access to the device. To review the execution results, follow these steps within your Hexnode UEM portal: Navigate to theAction Historytab under the specific device. Locate the entry for your PowerShell script (e.g., Disable Microsoft Accounts ) in theSubjectcolumn. Click theShow Outputbutton located to the status field.

title: Result Interpretation
content: The output can be interpreted using the following table:

title: Strategic Use cases
content: The following matrix outlines the strategic scenarios where these PowerShell scripts provide maximum business impact and operational security.

title: Operational Notes for Administrators
content: Error Handling:The scripts utilizeTry/Catchlogic to manage unexpected system errors (such as a busy database or restricted permissions). Instead of the script crashing, it will capture the error and display it in theShow Outputlog for easier troubleshooting. Active Session Handling:If a user is currently logged into a Microsoft Account, the Disable command may fail or require a systemrestartto fully terminate the session. PowerShell Execution Policy:While Hexnode UEM typically bypasses default restrictions, the script may fail to execute if a device is governed by a strictly enforced Restricted PowerShell execution policy. Scope of Impact:This script exclusively targets Microsoft Accounts that are currently present at the endpoint at the time of execution. It does not proactively prevent the addition of new accounts after the script has been successfully completed. Integrating these PowerShell scripts with Hexnode UEM enables IT departments to establish a rigorous and uniform identity management framework. By centralizing the control of Microsoft Account (MSA) accessibility, organizations can effectively eliminate unmanaged data synchronization and reinforce device security through enhanced administrative oversight. Share on: Share on:

content: Website blocklisting is a defense mechanism that prevents users from accessing harmful and unwanted web URLs. For instance, in the script given above, www.facebook.com , www.google.com and www.youtube.com are the URLs to be blocked.

title: How to install store apps on Windows devices - Hexnode Help Center
content: Help Center Deploying and Managing Apps Windows Category filter

title: How to install store apps on Windows devices
content: Installing and updating store apps on devices has always been a hefty job for the IT admins. Hexnode UEM supports a variety of application types and their deployment scenarios. To install a store app on your Windows devices, you can either push apps directly from the Microsoft Store or add apps to the app inventory to have them installed at a later stage. To distribute a store app, follow the steps mentioned below. Add store apps to the App inventory Distribute store apps to your devicesInstall store apps via ActionsInstall store apps via Policy Install store apps via Actions Install store apps via Policy

title: Add store apps to the App inventory
content: Login to your Hexnode UEM portal. Go toApps Add Apps Store App. Uncheck the optionsiOS,AndroidandmacOS. Type in the name of the required application on the box and click on. Click onSelect countryto get the apps from the app store for a particular country. ClickAddto select the required application. Now, the selected apps will be successfully added to your app inventory.

title: Distribute store apps to Windows devices
content: There are two ways by which you can distribute Store apps on your Windows devices. Install store apps via Action Install store apps via Policy

title: Install applications via Actions
content: Login to your Hexnode UEM portal. Navigate toManagetab and select the required devices. Click onActionsand chooseInstall Application. FromLocal Apps, uncheck iOS, Android, macOS, and tvOS to stop displaying the apps belonging to platforms other thanWindows. and select the required apps and click Done .OrSelectPublic Storeto install apps directly from the public app store and chooseWindows. and select the required apps and click Done . Login to your Hexnode UEM portal. Navigate toManagetab and click on the required device.This will take you to the corresponding device summary page. Click onActions Install Application. FromLocal Apps, and select the required apps and Click Done .OrSelectPublic Storeto install apps directly from the public app store. and select the required apps and Click Done .

title: Install applications via Policy
content: You can install store apps on your Windows devices using Required Apps policy. Login to your Hexnode UEM portal. Navigate toPoliciestab. Click onNew Policyto create a new one or click on any policy to edit an existing one. Enter thePolicy NameandDescriptionin the provided fields, if you are creating a new policy. Go toWindows App Management Required Apps Configure. Click on Add, choose eitherAdd ApporAdd Group. On selecting Add App, and select the required apps fromLocal Appsand click Done .OrSelectPublic Storeto install apps directly from the public app store. and select the required apps and click Done .On selecting Add Group, and select the required app groups and clickDone. and select the required apps fromLocal Appsand click Done . On selecting Add Group, and select the required app groups and clickDone. Navigate toPolicy Targets, and click onDevices, Device Groups, Users, User GroupsorDomains. Select the target entities. Click OK Save.

title: What happens at the device end?
content: Either you install the app via action or policy, the app installation proceeds silently in the background. The app can be easily accessed from theRecently addedsection of the Start . Share on: Share on:

title: How to enforce app installation on Windows devices - Hexnode Help Center
content: Help Center Deploying and Managing Apps Windows Category filter

title: How to enforce app installation on Windows devices
content: Most organizations require their workforce to install a number of essential applications on their devices. Hexnode UEM simplifies this process by remotely enforcing such apps to the devices by setting them as required. Defining required apps helps ensure that all the necessary applications remain installed silently on the devices. If a required app is missing on the device, the Hexnode UEM portal identifies it on the scheduled device scanand marks the device asnon-compliant. Resultantly, the portal will also reinitiate the app installation. You can easily deploy store and enterprise (MSI) apps on your Windows devices using the Required Apps policy. TheRequired Appspolicy is supported onWindows 10andWindows 11devices. To deploy enterprise apps via Required Apps, you must initiallyadd the app to the Hexnode app inventory.

title: Add Required apps to Windows devices via policy
content: To install apps silently using the Required Apps policy: Login to your Hexnode UEM portal Navigate to thePoliciestab Click onNew Policyto create a new one or click on any policy to edit an existing one Enter thePolicy NameandDescriptionin the provided fields. Go toWindows FromApp Management, chooseRequired Apps Click onConfigure. Click on the Addbutton choose either theAdd ApporAdd Groupoption. On choosingAdd App, and select the required apps fromLocal Apps. SelectPublic Storeto install apps directly from the public app store. and select the required apps.OrOn selectingAdd Group, and select the required app groups. On selectingAdd Group, and select the required app groups. When selecting the required app(s) or app group(s), clickConfigureunderCustom Installationto set upConfiguration Settingssuch as installation settings, uninstallation settings, or custom scripts for enterprise apps.Notes:Uninstallation settings are only available forMSIX/EXEapps.Only Windows 11 support MSI app installation on devices without the Hexnode agent app. Uninstallation settings are only available forMSIX/EXEapps. Only Windows 11 support MSI app installation on devices without the Hexnode agent app. ClickSaveto apply the settings. ClickDone. , associate the policy with target devices by navigating to thePolicy Targetstab. Select the requiredDevices/Device Groups/Users/User Groups/Domainsto which the policy is to be associated. Click onSave. An MSI app can have multiple versions added to the Hexnode app inventory. When included in an app group, admins can configure which version to use from within the app group. Any changes in the version of an MSI app in the app group updates it in the associatedRequired Appspolicy and on the device.

title: Installation Criteria for App Types with Multiple Versions
content: Hexnode UEM allows adding multiple versions of an app, both store and enterprise, to the app inventory. If multiple versions of an app are added to the Required Apps policy, only one version of the app will be deployed for installation on the devices. The priority of installation for different app versions depends on various cases that are explained below: If the enterprise app version is the same or higher than that of its store app version, the enterprise app version gets pushed to the devices. In case, if the store app version is higher than the enterprise app version, the store app version will be pushed to the devices. Share on: Share on:
